title: Charles Duans NGE Analysis
wikitext:
[]: Charles Duan's NGE Analysis             Quote Post by XX » Sun Oct 28, 2007 4:05 pm · Constructing the Self An analysis of Neon Genesis Evangelion [url]http://www.eecs.harvard.edu/~cduan/writings/eva.shtml[/url] "An analysis of Neon Genesis Evangelion and Kareshi Kanojo no Jijou, two anime series by Gainax Studios; written for a course during the spring term of 2001." · Supercomputers and Giant Robots An Exploration of Technology and the Human Soul in Neon Genesis Evangelion [url]http://www.eecs.harvard.edu/~cduan/writings/eva2.shtml[/url] "A second analysis paper on Evangelion, a bit shorter and simpler (no Freudian analysis). It's not as good as the previous one (in my opinion), but it's still pretty interesting--these two research papers are among the few that I still read every once in a while." - Charles Duan , Student of Harvard Law School. Essays extracted from the Harvard Electrical Engineering & Computer Science (EECS) Faculty Website [url]http://www.eecs.harvard.edu/index/eecs_faculty.php[/url] I [XX] have not written this documents, only posted it here

[]:             Quote Post by BrikHaus » Sun Oct 28, 2007 4:15 pm I wish I had the free time to read these.

[]:             Quote Post by CitizenGeek » Sun Oct 28, 2007 4:40 pm I haven't seen Kare Kano, so the first essay doesn't really interest me at the moment. However, I have just finished the significatly shorter second essay (Supercomputers and Giant Robots) and I'm very impressed! Your writing was brilliant. It was a fantastic exploration of one of Eva's central themes and I highly reccomend that the rest of you read it! What did the teacher that corrected this essay make of it?

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Oct 28, 2007 5:31 pm I have bookmarked these and will read ASAP. They do look most interesting. It's always nice to see NGE getting attention to those outside the hardcore anime community.

[]:             Quote Post by BrikHaus » Sun Oct 28, 2007 6:42 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: I have bookmarked these and will read ASAP. They do look most interesting. It's always nice to see NGE getting attention to those outside the hardcore anime community.

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Sun Oct 28, 2007 6:44 pm I remember those. http://www.evamonkey.com/writings_duan01.php http://www.evamonkey.com/writings_duan02.php

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Mon Oct 29, 2007 7:13 am BrikHaus wrote: How do you know he's outside the hardcore anime community? Maybe he's really MaggotMaster.

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Mon Oct 29, 2007 3:51 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: BrikHaus wrote: How do you know he's outside the hardcore anime community? Maybe he's really MaggotMaster.

[]:             Quote Post by Shawn Dudley » Mon Oct 29, 2007 5:46 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: BrikHaus wrote: How do you know he's outside the hardcore anime community? Maybe he's really MaggotMaster. True. But if he's really going to Harvard then we know he's got more going for him than just being an obsessed Otaku.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Mon Oct 29, 2007 7:14 pm Ornette wrote: A good friend of mine went to Harvard for Neurosurgery, and he was a hardcore otaku, was even a member of the anime club there. He was one of the people that got me into anime back in the early nineties.

[]:             Quote Post by BrikHaus » Mon Oct 29, 2007 7:25 pm I didn't realize that liking anime excluded you from doing cool stuff like becoming a doctor.* *Disclaimer: Being a doctor is not cool.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Mon Oct 29, 2007 7:30 pm It doesn't, it doesn't! I surrender! I raise my tiny little white flag! I'm merely saying that anime often doesn't get much respect outside of its own community for various reasons. One is because there aren't enough respectable champions of it. One reason I've always appreciated Roger Ebert is because he's always promoted anime as an equally valid film medium.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Oct 30, 2007 3:22 pm *intentional double post, allow me one* I just read both and I am mighty impressed. There are a few minor quibbles, but overall they are superb theses. In Supercomputers I must correct you though and say that Naoko's soul is not within the Magi but merely her brain waves. Her self as a mother, woman, and scientist to be specific (I also think there's a lot of subtext behind that division). While the Eva's were appropriate vessels that could house souls, I think they wanted to make it clear that machines like the Magi could not house souls like Eva's could. This (somewhat) mirrors the Dummy Plug System where they tried to copy human thought patterns to trick the Evas but they couldn't digitize the human soul. With structured self Vs. real self I'd say the dichotomy is not as clearly defined. First, there's the idea that none of us are brought up free from nurture or nature. We all owe a very large amount (and it's a matter for people better than myself to determine how much) to our environments/culture and those who raise us in determining who we are. I'll leave it there for now, but overall, quite excellent.

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Tue Oct 30, 2007 4:16 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: Ornette wrote: A good friend of mine went to Harvard for Neurosurgery, and he was a hardcore otaku, was even a member of the anime club there. He was one of the people that got me into anime back in the early nineties.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Oct 30, 2007 4:27 pm No problem. Damn online ambiguity. *Shakes fist in exaggerated gesture at heavens so as to be crystal clear that I'm being being verily not serious*
